Case title: Agentic title/abstract screening in migration-policy reviews with gray-literature rescue
Short description: Test AI-first screening on mixed corpora to see whether first-pass screening shrinks while protocol design and corpus rescue remain human.
Empirical unit of analysis: Review records, inclusion decisions, and rescue decisions across indexed and gray sources
Observable human actors: Review lead, RA screeners, librarian-equivalent search designer
Candidate roles tested: Protocol and provenance architect; corpus pluralist; shrinking first-pass screener
Key typology question: Has first-pass screening become a shrinking task-cluster rather than a durable role?
Observable artifacts / traces: PRISMA logs, search paths, inclusion/exclusion reasons, rescued-study logs
Key outcomes observed: Recall, precision, workload saved, conclusion sensitivity to rescued studies
What would weaken / falsify the role cla: AI-first workflows preserve gray and multilingual evidence without human rescue
Main confounds / risks of misreading: Access to subscriptions and topic difficulty can mimic AI effects
Why this case has high leverage: It tests both shrinkage and durable boundary-setting in one workflow.
collector_notes: Generated from workbook and workbook-linked sources.
